html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirteen — Part Three

The Book of Shemu'el Bet

SHEMU'EL BET

The Second Book of Samuel

Chapters 13 through 18

Tamar — Avshalom's Rebellion — Dawid's Flight — The Battle in the Forest

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QSama (4Q51) — Cave 4

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTERS 13 THROUGH 18 — AVSHALOM'S REBELLION

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 13 Amnon and Tamar — Avshalom Kills Amnon — Avshalom Flees to Geshur

1 Afterward this happened: Dawid's son Avshalom had a beautiful sister, whose name was Tamar. And Amnon, Dawid's son, loved her.

2 And Amnon was so tormented that he made himself ill because of his sister Tamar, for she was a virgin, and it seemed impossible to Amnon to do anything to her.

3 But Amnon had a friend, whose name was Yonadav, the son of Shim'ah, Dawid's brother. And Yonadav was a very crafty man.

4 And he said to him, O son of the king, why are you so haggard morning after morning? Will you not tell me? Amnon said to him, I love Tamar, my brother Avshalom's sister.

5 Yonadav said to him, Lie down on your bed and pretend to be ill. And when your father comes to see you, say to him, Let my sister Tamar come and give me bread to eat, and prepare the food in my sight, that I may see it and eat it from her hand.

6 So Amnon lay down and pretended to be ill. And when the king came to see him, Amnon said to the king, Please let my sister Tamar come and make a couple of cakes in my sight, that I may eat from her hand.

7 Then Dawid sent home to Tamar, saying, Go to your brother Amnon's house and prepare food for him.

8 So Tamar went to her brother Amnon's house, where he was lying down. And she took dough and kneaded it and made cakes in his sight and baked the cakes.

9 And she took the pan and emptied it out before him, but he refused to eat. And Amnon said, Send out everyone from me. So everyone went out from him.

10 Then Amnon said to Tamar, Bring the food into the chamber, that I may eat from your hand. And Tamar took the cakes she had made and brought them into the chamber to Amnon her brother.

11 But when she brought them near him to eat, he took hold of her and said to her, Come, lie with me, my sister.

12 She answered him, No, my brother, do not violate me, for such a thing is not done in Yisra'EL — do not do this outrageous thing.

13 As for me, where could I carry my shame? And as for you, you would be as one of the outrageous fools in Yisra'EL. Now therefore, please speak to the king, for he will not withhold me from you.

14 But he would not listen to her, and being stronger than she, he violated her and lay with her.

15 Then Amnon hated her with very great hatred, so that the hatred with which he hated her was greater than the love with which he had loved her. And Amnon said to her, Get up and go.

16 But she said to him, No, my brother, for this wrong in sending me away is greater than the other that you did to me. But he would not listen to her.

17 He called the young man who served him and said, Put this woman out of my presence and bolt the door after her.

18 Now she was wearing a long robe with sleeves, for thus were the virgin daughters of the king dressed. So his servant put her out and bolted the door after her.

19 And Tamar put ashes on her head and tore the long robe that she wore. And she laid her hand on her head and went away, crying aloud as she went.

20 And her brother Avshalom said to her, Has Amnon your brother been with you? Now hold your peace, my sister. He is your brother — do not take this to heart. So Tamar lived, a desolate woman, in her brother Avshalom's house.

21 When King Dawid heard of all these things, he was very angry.

22 But Avshalom spoke to Amnon neither good nor bad, for Avshalom hated Amnon, because he had violated his sister Tamar.

23 After two full years Avshalom had sheepshearers at Ba'al-Khatzor, which is near Efrayim, and Avshalom invited all the king's sons.

24 And Avshalom came to the king and said, Behold, your servant has sheepshearers. Please let the king and his servants go with your servant.

25 But the king said to Avshalom, No, my son, let us not all go, lest we be burdensome to you. He pressed him, but he would not go but gave him his blessing.

26 Then Avshalom said, If not, please let my brother Amnon go with us. And the king said to him, Why should he go with you?

27 But Avshalom pressed him until he let Amnon and all the king's sons go with him.

28 Then Avshalom commanded his servants, Mark when Amnon's heart is merry with wine, and when I say to you, Strike Amnon, then kill him. Do not fear — have I not commanded you? Be courageous and be valiant.

29 So the servants of Avshalom did to Amnon as Avshalom had commanded. Then all the king's sons arose, and each mounted his mule and fled.

30 While they were on the way, news came to Dawid, Avshalom has struck down all the king's sons, and not one of them is left.

31 Then the king arose and tore his garments and lay on the earth. And all his servants who were standing by tore their garments.

32 But Yonadav, the son of Shim'ah, Dawid's brother, said, Let not my lord suppose that they have killed all the young men, the king's sons, for Amnon alone is dead. For by the command of Avshalom this has been determined from the day he violated his sister Tamar.

33 Now therefore let not my lord the king take it to heart as if all the king's sons are dead, for Amnon alone is dead.

34 But Avshalom fled. And the young man who kept the watch lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, many people were coming from the road behind him by the side of the mountain.

35 And Yonadav said to the king, Behold, the king's sons have come — as your servant said, so it has come about.

36 And as soon as he had finished speaking, behold, the king's sons came and lifted up their voice and wept. And the king also and all his servants wept very bitterly.

37 But Avshalom fled and went to Talmai the son of Ammihud, king of Geshur. And Dawid mourned for his son day after day.

38 So Avshalom fled and went to Geshur, and was there three years.

39 And the spirit of the king longed to go out to Avshalom, because he was comforted about Amnon, since he was dead.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 13. The violation of Tamar is the covenant consequence YHWH declared in Chapter 12:11 — I will raise up evil against you out of your own house. The Nathan oracle's judgment begins to unfold within Dawid's own family. The sword that never departs begins with Amnon's violence.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 13 in full. Tamar's words — verse 12-13 — such a thing is not done in Yisra'EL, do not do this outrageous thing — are read as the covenant voice of covenant law speaking in the face of covenant violation. She identifies the act by its covenant character: outrageous, impossible in Yisra'EL.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 13 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Verse 21 — when King Dawid heard of all these things, he was very angry — but he did not punish Amnon. The 4QSama text adds: but he did not grieve the spirit of Amnon his son, because he loved him, for he was his firstborn. This addition explains Dawid's inaction and is included in some modern critical editions.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 15 — then Amnon hated her with very great hatred, so that the hatred with which he hated her was greater than the love with which he had loved her. The covenant psychology of lust and its aftermath is described with brutal precision: what called itself love was only desire. When desire was satisfied, what remained was hatred. Verse 20 — Tamar lived, a desolate woman, in her brother Avshalom's house. The single most heartbreaking line in the chapter. The covenant community that should have protected her did not. The king who should have executed covenant justice did not. She lives as a desolate woman — the Hebrew shomemah — destroyed, laid waste. The same word used for devastated cities.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 14 The Wise Woman of Teko'a — Avshalom Returns to Yerushalayim — Dawid Kisses Avshalom

1 Now Yoav the son of Tzeruya knew that the king's heart went out to Avshalom.

2 And Yoav sent to Teko'a and brought from there a wise woman and said to her, Please pretend to be a mourner and put on mourning garments. Do not anoint yourself with oil, but behave like a woman who has been mourning many days for the dead.

3 Go to the king and speak thus to him. So Yoav put the words in her mouth.

4 When the woman of Teko'a came to the king, she fell on her face to the ground and paid homage and said, Help, O king.

5 And the king said to her, What is your trouble? She answered, Alas, I am a widow — my husband is dead.

6 And your servant had two sons, and they quarreled with one another in the field. There was no one to separate them, and one struck the other and killed him.

7 And now the whole clan has risen against your servant, and they say, Give up the man who struck his brother, that we may put him to death for the life of his brother whom he killed. And so they would destroy the heir also. Thus they would quench my coal that is left and leave to my husband neither name nor remnant on the face of the earth.

8 Then the king said to the woman, Go to your house, and I will give orders concerning you.

9 And the woman of Teko'a said to the king, On me be the guilt, my lord the king, and on my father's house — let the king and his throne be guiltless.

10 The king said, If anyone says anything to you, bring him to me, and he shall never touch you again.

11 Then she said, Please let the king invoke YHWH your Creator, that the avenger of blood kill no more, and my son not be destroyed. He said, As YHWH lives, not one hair of your son shall fall to the ground.

12 Then the woman said, Please let your servant speak a word to my lord the king. He said, Speak.

13 The woman said, Why then have you planned such a thing against the people of Creator? For in giving this decision the king convicts himself, inasmuch as the king does not bring his banished one home again.

14 We must all die — we are like water spilled on the ground, which cannot be gathered up again. But Creator will not take away life, and he devises means so that the banished one will not remain an outcast.

15 Now I have come to say this to my lord the king because the people have made me afraid, and your servant thought, I will speak to the king. It may be that the king will perform the request of his servant.

16 For the king will hear and deliver his servant from the hand of the man who would destroy me and my son together from the heritage of Creator.

17 And your servant thought, The word of my lord the king will set me at rest, for my lord the king is like the angel of Creator to discern good and evil. May YHWH your Creator be with you.

18 Then the king answered the woman, Do not hide from me anything I ask you. And the woman said, Let my lord the king speak.

19 The king said, Is the hand of Yoav with you in all this? The woman answered and said, As your soul lives, my lord the king, one cannot turn to the right hand or to the left from anything that my lord the king has said. It was your servant Yoav who commanded me — it was he who put all these words in the mouth of your servant.

20 In order to change the course of things your servant Yoav did this. But my lord has wisdom like the wisdom of the angel of Creator to know all things that are on earth.

21 Then the king said to Yoav, Behold now, I do this thing. Go, bring back the young man Avshalom.

22 And Yoav fell on his face to the ground and paid homage and blessed the king. And Yoav said, Today your servant knows that I have found favor in your sight, my lord the king, in that the king has granted the request of his servant.

23 So Yoav arose and went to Geshur and brought Avshalom to Yerushalayim.

24 And the king said, Let him dwell apart in his own house — he is not to come into my presence. So Avshalom lived apart in his own house and did not come into the king's presence.

25 Now in all Yisra'EL there was no one so much to be praised for his handsome appearance as Avshalom. From the sole of his foot to the crown of his head there was no blemish in him.

26 And when he cut the hair of his head — he cut it at the end of every year, because it was heavy on him, and when he cut it he weighed the hair of his head, two hundred shekels by the king's weight —

27 There were born to Avshalom three sons, and one daughter whose name was Tamar. She was a beautiful woman.

28 So Avshalom lived two full years in Yerushalayim without coming into the king's presence.

29 Then Avshalom sent for Yoav, to send him to the king, but Yoav would not come to him. And he sent a second time, but Yoav would not come.

30 Then he said to his servants, See, Yoav's field is next to mine, and he has barley there — go and set it on fire. So Avshalom's servants set the field on fire.

31 Then Yoav arose and went to Avshalom at his house and said to him, Why have your servants set my field on fire?

32 Avshalom answered Yoav, Behold, I sent word to you, Come here, that I may send you to the king, to ask, Why have I come from Geshur? It would be better for me to be there still. Now therefore let me go into the presence of the king, and if there is guilt in me, let him put me to death.

33 Then Yoav went to the king and told him, and he summoned Avshalom. So he came to the king and bowed himself on his face to the ground before the king, and the king kissed Avshalom.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 14. The wise woman of Teko'a presents a parable — like Natan in Chapter 12 — that causes the king to pronounce covenant mercy before applying it to himself. The parable is Yoav's construction but the wisdom is covenant wisdom: Creator devises means so that the banished one will not remain an outcast — verse 14.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 14 in full. Verse 14 — we must all die, we are like water spilled on the ground, which cannot be gathered up again. But Creator will not take away life, and he devises means so that the banished one will not remain an outcast — is read as a covenant declaration of YHWH's character: his disposition is toward restoration, not permanent exclusion.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 14 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Dawid's two-year refusal to see Avshalom — verse 28 — after bringing him home reveals the incomplete nature of the reconciliation. Avshalom is physically present but relationally excluded. The half-restoration produces worse consequences than either full reconciliation or continued exile.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 33 — the king kissed Avshalom. The reconciliation scene is brief, formal, and inadequate. Two years of isolation, a burning of Yoav's field to force the meeting, a prostration and a kiss. No words of covenant restoration are recorded. No acknowledgment of the murder of Amnon. No genuine reckoning. The half-measure reconciliation planted the seed of the full rebellion that follows in Chapter 15. Dawid's failure to exercise covenant justice — twice — once with Amnon, once with Avshalom — produces the sword that will not depart from his house.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 15 Avshalom Steals the Hearts of Yisra'EL — Dawid Flees Yerushalayim — The Ark Sent Back

1 After this Avshalom got himself a chariot and horses, and fifty men to run before him.

2 And Avshalom used to rise early and stand beside the way of the gate. And when any man had a dispute to come before the king for judgment, Avshalom would call to him and say, From what city are you? And when he said, Your servant is of such and such a tribe in Yisra'EL,

3 Avshalom would say to him, See, your claims are good and right, but there is no man designated by the king to hear you.

4 Then Avshalom would say, Oh that I were judge in the land. Then every man with a dispute or cause might come to me, and I would give him justice.

5 And whenever a man came near to pay homage to him, he would put out his hand and take hold of him and kiss him.

6 Thus Avshalom did to all of Yisra'EL who came to the king for judgment. So Avshalom stole the hearts of the men of Yisra'EL.

7 And at the end of four years Avshalom said to the king, Please let me go and pay my vow, which I have vowed to YHWH, in Khevron.

8 For your servant vowed a vow while I lived at Geshur in Aram, saying, If YHWH will indeed bring me back to Yerushalayim, then I will offer worship to YHWH.

9 The king said to him, Go in peace. So he arose and went to Khevron.

10 But Avshalom sent secret messengers throughout all the tribes of Yisra'EL, saying, As soon as you hear the sound of the trumpet, then say, Avshalom is king at Khevron.

11 With Avshalom went two hundred men from Yerushalayim who were invited guests, and they went in their innocence and knew nothing.

12 And while Avshalom was offering the sacrifices, he sent for Akhitofel the Gilonite, Dawid's counselor, from his city Gilo. And the conspiracy grew strong, and the people with Avshalom kept increasing.

13 And a messenger came to Dawid, saying, The hearts of the men of Yisra'EL have gone after Avshalom.

14 Then Dawid said to all his servants who were with him at Yerushalayim, Arise, and let us flee, or else there will be no escape for us from Avshalom. Go quickly, lest he overtake us quickly and bring down ruin on us and strike the city with the edge of the sword.

15 And the king's servants said to the king, Behold, your servants are ready to do whatever my lord the king decides.

16 So the king went out, and all his household after him. And the king left ten concubines to keep the house.

17 And the king went out, and all the people after him. And they halted at the last house.

18 And all his servants passed by him, and all the Kerethites, and all the Pelethites, and all the six hundred Gittites who had followed him from Gat, passed before the king.

19 Then the king said to Ittai the Gittite, Why do you also go with us? Go back and stay with the king, for you are a foreigner and also an exile from your home.

20 You came only yesterday, and shall I today make you wander with us, since I go I know not where? Go back and take your brothers with you, and may YHWH show steadfast love and faithfulness to you.

21 But Ittai answered the king, As YHWH lives, and as my lord the king lives, wherever my lord the king shall be, whether in death or life, even there also will your servant be.

22 And Dawid said to Ittai, Go then, pass on. So Ittai the Gittite passed on with all his men and all the little ones who were with him.

23 And all the land wept aloud as all the people passed by, and the king crossed the brook Kidron, and all the people passed on toward the wilderness.

24 And Zadok came also with all the Levites, bearing the Ark of the Covenant of Creator. And they set down the Ark of Creator until the people had all passed out of the city. And Evyatar offered sacrifices until the people had all passed out of the city.

25 Then the king said to Tzadok, Carry the Ark of Creator back into the city. If I find favor in the eyes of YHWH, he will bring me back and let me see both it and his dwelling place.

26 But if he says, I have no pleasure in you, behold, here I am — let him do to me what seems good to him.

27 The king also said to Tzadok the priest, Are you not a seer? Go back to the city in peace, with your two sons, Akhima'atz your son, and Yehonatan the son of Evyatar.

28 See, I will wait at the fords of the wilderness until word comes from you to inform me.

29 So Tzadok and Evyatar carried the Ark of Creator back to Yerushalayim, and they remained there.

30 But Dawid went up the ascent of the Mount of Olives, weeping as he went, barefoot and with his head covered. And all the people who were with him covered their heads, and they went up, weeping as they went.

31 And it was told Dawid, Akhitofel is among the conspirators with Avshalom. And Dawid said, O YHWH, please turn the counsel of Akhitofel into foolishness.

32 When Dawid came to the summit, where Creator was worshiped, behold, Khushai the Arkite came to meet him with his coat torn and dirt on his head.

33 Dawid said to him, If you go on with me, you will be a burden to me.

34 But if you return to the city and say to Avshalom, I will be your servant, O king — as I have been your father's servant in time past, so now I will be your servant — then you will defeat for me the counsel of Akhitofel.

35 Are not Tzadok and Evyatar the priests with you there? So whatever you hear from the king's house, tell it to Tzadok and Evyatar the priests.

36 Behold, their two sons are with them there, Akhima'atz, Tzadok's son, and Yehonatan, Evyatar's son, and by them you shall send to me everything you hear.

37 So Khushai, Dawid's friend, came into the city, just as Avshalom was entering Yerushalayim.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 15. Dawid's flight from Yerushalayim is the fulfillment of the Nathan oracle — verse 12:11 — I will raise up evil against you out of your own house. The covenant king flees his own city because of his own son. The Ark sent back to Yerushalayim — verses 25-26 — is Dawid's most profound covenant act in exile: he refuses to use the Ark as a talisman or political instrument.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 15 in full. Verse 25-26 — carry the Ark back into the city — if I find favor in the eyes of YHWH, he will bring me back — but if he says, I have no pleasure in you, behold, here I am — let him do what seems good to him — is read as the highest expression of covenant submission in the entire Dawid narrative: total surrender to YHWH's disposition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 15 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Ittai the Gittite's declaration — verse 21 — wherever my lord the king shall be, whether in death or life, even there also will your servant be — echoes the covenant loyalty formula of Ruth 1:16-17. The foreigner again demonstrates covenant loyalty exceeding that of the native born.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 25-26 — Dawid's command to send the Ark back is one of the most theologically significant acts in Shemu'el Bet. The man who danced before the Ark with all his might — Chapter 6 — now refuses to take it into exile as a covenant guarantee. He submits the question of his return entirely to YHWH's pleasure. If YHWH wants him back, YHWH will bring him back — without the Ark as leverage. Verse 30 — Dawid went up the Mount of Olives, weeping, barefoot, head covered. The covenant king in his deepest humiliation. The Psalms of the ascents — the covenant songs of pilgrimage — will be sung on this same mountain by every generation that follows.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 16 Tziva's Lie — Shim'i Curses Dawid — Akhitofel Counsels Avshalom

1 When Dawid had passed a little beyond the summit, Tziva the servant of Mefiboshet met him, with a couple of donkeys saddled, bearing two hundred loaves of bread, a hundred bunches of raisins, a hundred of summer fruits, and a skin of wine.

2 And the king said to Tziva, Why have you brought these? Tziva answered, The donkeys are for the king's household to ride on, the bread and summer fruit for the young men to eat, and the wine for those who faint in the wilderness to drink.

3 And the king said, And where is your master's son? Tziva said to the king, Behold, he remains in Yerushalayim, for he said, Today the house of Yisra'EL will give me back the kingdom of my father.

4 Then the king said to Tziva, Behold, all that belonged to Mefiboshet is now yours. And Tziva said, I pay homage — let me ever find favor in your sight, my lord the king.

5 When King Dawid came to Bakhurim, there came out a man of the family of the house of Sha'ul, whose name was Shim'i, the son of Gera, and as he came he cursed continually.

6 And he threw stones at Dawid and at all the servants of King Dawid, and all the people and all the mighty men were on his right hand and on his left.

7 And Shim'i said as he cursed, Get out, get out, you man of blood, you worthless man.

8 YHWH has avenged on you all the blood of the house of Sha'ul, in whose place you have reigned, and YHWH has given the kingdom into the hand of your son Avshalom. See, your evil is on you, for you are a man of blood.

9 Then Avishai the son of Tzeruya said to the king, Why should this dead dog curse my lord the king? Let me go over and take off his head.

10 But the king said, What have I to do with you, you sons of Tzeruya? If he is cursing because YHWH said to him, Curse Dawid, who then shall say, Why have you done so?

11 And Dawid said to Avishai and to all his servants, Behold, my son who came from my own body seeks my life — how much more now may this Binyaminite. Leave him alone, and let him curse, for YHWH has told him to.

12 It may be that YHWH will look on the wrong done to me, and that YHWH will repay me with good for his cursing today.

13 So Dawid and his men went on the road, while Shim'i went along on the hillside opposite him and cursed as he went and threw stones at him and flung dust.

14 And the king, and all the people who were with him, arrived weary at the Jordan. And there he refreshed himself.

15 Now Avshalom and all the people, the men of Yisra'EL, came to Yerushalayim, and Akhitofel with him.

16 And when Khushai the Arkite, Dawid's friend, came to Avshalom, Khushai said to Avshalom, Long live the king. Long live the king.

17 And Avshalom said to Khushai, Is this your loyalty to your friend? Why did you not go with your friend?

18 And Khushai said to Avshalom, No, for whom YHWH and this people and all the men of Yisra'EL have chosen, his I will be, and with him I will remain.

19 And again, whom should I serve? Should it not be his son? As I have served your father, so I will serve you.

20 Then Avshalom said to Akhitofel, Give your counsel. What shall we do?

21 Akhitofel said to Avshalom, Go in to your father's concubines, whom he has left to keep the house, and all Yisra'EL will hear that you have made yourself a stench to your father, and the hands of all who are with you will be strengthened.

22 So they pitched a tent for Avshalom on the roof. And Avshalom went in to his father's concubines in the sight of all Yisra'EL.

23 Now in those days the counsel that Akhitofel gave was as if one consulted the word of Creator — so was all the counsel of Akhitofel esteemed, both by Dawid and by Avshalom.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 16. Verse 21-22 — Avshalom goes in to his father's concubines on the roof, in the sight of all Yisra'EL — is the precise fulfillment of the Nathan oracle in Chapter 12:11-12: I will take your wives before your eyes and give them to your neighbor — for you did it secretly, but I will do this thing before all Yisra'EL and before the sun.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 16 in full. Dawid's response to Shim'i's cursing — verse 10-12 — is read as the covenant model of humility under accusation. He does not defend himself. He leaves room for YHWH to look on the wrong done to him and to repay according to his own justice.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 16 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Akhitofel's counsel — verse 23 — was as if one consulted the word of Creator. The most celebrated human counselor in Dawid's court is now counseling his enemy. The covenant intelligence that served the kingdom now serves its destruction.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10-12 — Dawid's response to Shim'i is the covenant theology of the fleeing king at its most profound. Shim'i's accusations are false — Dawid did not murder the house of Sha'ul. But Dawid does not defend himself. He identifies the possibility that YHWH is using even the false accusation: it may be that YHWH will look on the wrong done to me and repay me with good for his cursing today. The man who is being falsely cursed while fleeing for his life leaves room for YHWH to be the judge rather than claiming that right himself. This is the covenant disposition that distinguishes Dawid from every other king in the Nevi'im.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 17 Akhitofel's Counsel Defeated — Khushai's Strategy — YHWH Had Ordained to Defeat the Good Counsel

1 Moreover, Akhitofel said to Avshalom, Let me choose twelve thousand men, and I will arise and pursue Dawid tonight.

2 I will come upon him while he is weary and discouraged and throw him into a panic, and all the people who are with him will flee. I will strike down only the king,

3 And will bring all the people back to you as a bride comes home to her husband. You seek the life of only one man, and all the people will be at peace.

4 And the advice seemed right in the eyes of Avshalom and all the elders of Yisra'EL.

5 Then Avshalom said, Call Khushai the Arkite also, and let us hear what he has to say.

6 And when Khushai came to Avshalom, Avshalom said to him, Thus has Akhitofel spoken. Shall we do as he says? If not, you speak.

7 Then Khushai said to Avshalom, This time the counsel that Akhitofel has given is not good.

8 Khushai said, You know that your father and his men are mighty men, and that they are enraged, like a bear robbed of her cubs in the field. Besides, your father is expert in war — he will not spend the night with the people.

9 Behold, even now he has hidden himself in one of the pits or in some other place. And as soon as some of the people fall at the first attack, whoever hears it will say, There has been a defeat among the people who follow Avshalom.

10 Then even the valiant man, whose heart is like the heart of a lion, will utterly melt with fear, for all Yisra'EL knows that your father is a mighty man, and that those who are with him are valiant men.

11 But my counsel is that all Yisra'EL be gathered to you, from Dan to Be'er-Sheva, as the sand by the sea for multitude, and that you go to battle in person.

12 So we shall come upon him in some place where he is to be found, and we shall light on him as the dew falls on the ground, and of him and all the men with him not one will be left.

13 If he withdraws into a city, then all Yisra'EL shall bring ropes to that city, and we shall drag it into the valley, until not even a pebble is to be found there.

14 And Avshalom and all the men of Yisra'EL said, The counsel of Khushai the Arkite is better than the counsel of Akhitofel. For YHWH had ordained to defeat the good counsel of Akhitofel, so that YHWH might bring harm upon Avshalom.

15 Then Khushai said to Tzadok and Evyatar the priests, Thus and so did Akhitofel counsel Avshalom and the elders of Yisra'EL, and thus and so have I counseled.

16 Now therefore send quickly and tell Dawid, Do not stay tonight at the fords of the wilderness, but by all means pass over, lest the king and all the people who are with him be swallowed up.

17 Now Yehonatan and Akhima'atz were waiting at En-Rogel. A female servant was to go and tell them, and they were to go and tell King Dawid, for they were not to be seen entering the city.

18 But a young man saw them and told Avshalom. So both of them went away quickly and came to the house of a man at Bakhurim, who had a well in his courtyard. And they went down into it.

19 And the woman took and spread a covering over the well's mouth and scattered grain on it, and nothing was known of it.

20 When Avshalom's servants came to the woman at the house, they said, Where are Akhima'atz and Yehonatan? And the woman said to them, They have gone over the brook of water. And when they had sought and could not find them, they returned to Yerushalayim.

21 After they had gone, the men came up out of the well, and went and told King Dawid. They said to Dawid, Arise, and go quickly over the water, for thus and so has Akhitofel counseled against you.

22 Then Dawid arose, and all the people who were with him, and they crossed the Yarden. By daybreak not one was left who had not crossed the Yarden.

23 When Akhitofel saw that his counsel was not followed, he saddled his donkey and went off home to his own city. He set his house in order and hanged himself, and he died and was buried in the tomb of his father.

24 Then Dawid came to Makhanayim. And Avshalom crossed the Yarden with all the men of Yisra'EL.

25 Now Avshalom had set Amasa over the army instead of Yoav. Amasa was the son of a man named Yitra the Ishmaelite, who had married Avigal the daughter of Nakash, sister of Tzeruya, Yoav's mother.

26 And Yisra'EL and Avshalom encamped in the land of Gil'ad.

27 When Dawid came to Makhanayim, Shobi the son of Nakhash from Rabbah of the Ammonites, and Makhir the son of Ammiel from Lo-Devar, and Barzillai the Gil'adite from Rogelim,

28 Brought beds, basins, and earthen vessels, wheat, barley, flour, parched grain, beans and lentils,

29 Honey and curds and sheep and cheese from the herd, for Dawid and the people with him to eat, for they said, The people are hungry and weary and thirsty in the wilderness.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 17. Verse 14 — for YHWH had ordained to defeat the good counsel of Akhitofel, so that YHWH might bring harm upon Avshalom — is the covenant declaration of divine sovereignty within human political deliberation. Avshalom chose Khushai's inferior counsel for entirely human reasons, and YHWH was working through the choice.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 17 in full. Akhitofel's suicide — verse 23 — is read as the covenant consequence of placing one's entire identity in the exercise of counsel: when the counsel is rejected, there is nothing left. The counselor who does not serve YHWH has no ground to stand on when his wisdom fails.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 17 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Akhitofel's counsel — verses 1-3 — was actually superior strategy. Khushai's counter-counsel was militarily weaker but psychologically effective. YHWH worked through Avshalom's vanity — wanting a mass army with himself leading it — to save Dawid.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 14 — YHWH had ordained to defeat the good counsel of Akhitofel. The text does not say Khushai's counsel was better military strategy. It says YHWH ordained the defeat of the better strategy. YHWH works within Avshalom's pride — the desire for a great army with Avshalom at the head — to produce the outcome Dawid prayed for in Chapter 15:31 — O YHWH, please turn the counsel of Akhitofel into foolishness. The prayer is answered through Avshalom's own character flaw. Verse 23 — Akhitofel hanged himself. The only suicide recorded in Shemu'el. The man whose counsel was as the word of Creator dies when his counsel is overridden by YHWH through the inferior counter-counsel of a double agent.

SHEMU'EL BET — CHAPTER 18 The Battle in the Forest of Efrayim — Avshalom Killed by Yoav — O Avshalom My Son

1 Then Dawid mustered the men who were with him and set over them commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds.

2 And Dawid sent out the army, one third under the command of Yoav, one third under the command of Avishai the son of Tzeruya, Yoav's brother, and one third under the command of Ittai the Gittite. And the king said to the men, I myself will also go out with you.

3 But the men said, You shall not go out. For if we flee, they will not care about us. If half of us die, they will not care about us. But you are worth ten thousand of us. Therefore it is better that you send us help from the city.

4 The king said to them, Whatever seems best to you I will do. So the king stood at the side of the gate, while all the army marched out by hundreds and by thousands.

5 And the king commanded Yoav and Avishai and Ittai, Deal gently for my sake with the young man Avshalom. And all the people heard when the king gave orders to all the commanders about Avshalom.

6 So the army went out into the field against Yisra'EL, and the battle was fought in the forest of Efrayim.

7 And the men of Yisra'EL were defeated there before the servants of Dawid, and the loss there was great on that day — twenty thousand men.

8 The battle spread over the face of all the country, and the forest devoured more people that day than the sword.

9 And Avshalom happened to meet the servants of Dawid. Avshalom was riding on his mule, and the mule went under the thick branches of a great oak, and his head caught fast in the oak, and he was suspended between heaven and earth, while the mule that was under him went on.

10 And a certain man saw it and told Yoav, Behold, I saw Avshalom hanging in an oak.

11 Yoav said to the man who told him, What, you saw him? Why then did you not strike him there to the ground? I would have been glad to give you ten pieces of silver and a belt.

12 But the man said to Yoav, Even if I felt in my hand the weight of a thousand pieces of silver, I would not reach out my hand against the king's son, for in our hearing the king commanded you and Avishai and Ittai, saying, For my sake protect the young man Avshalom.

13 On the other hand, if I had dealt treacherously against his life — and there is nothing hidden from the king — then you yourself would have stood aloof.

14 Yoav said, I will not waste time like this with you. And he took three javelins in his hand and thrust them into the heart of Avshalom while he was still alive in the oak.

15 And ten young men, Yoav's armor-bearers, surrounded Avshalom and struck him and killed him.

16 Then Yoav blew the trumpet, and the troops came back from pursuing Yisra'EL, for Yoav restrained the troops.

17 And they took Avshalom and threw him into a great pit in the forest and raised over him a very great heap of stones. And all Yisra'EL fled every one to his own home.

18 Now Avshalom in his lifetime had taken and set up for himself the pillar that is in the King's Valley, for he said, I have no son to keep my name in remembrance. He called the pillar after his own name, and it is called Avshalom's monument to this day.

19 Then Akhima'atz the son of Tzadok said, Let me run and carry news to the king that YHWH has delivered him from the hand of his enemies.

20 And Yoav said to him, You are not to carry news today. You may carry news another day, but today you shall carry no news, because the king's son is dead.

21 Then Yoav said to the Kushite, Go, tell the king what you have seen. The Kushite bowed before Yoav and ran.

22 Then Akhima'atz the son of Tzadok said again to Yoav, Come what may, let me also run after the Kushite. And Yoav said, Why will you run, my son, seeing that you will have no reward for the news?

23 Come what may, I will run. And he said to him, Run. Then Akhima'atz ran by the way of the plain and outran the Kushite.

24 Now Dawid was sitting between the two gates. And the watchman went up to the roof of the gate by the wall and lifted up his eyes and looked, and behold, a man running alone.

25 And the watchman called out and told the king. And the king said, If he is alone, there is news in his mouth. And he came rapidly and drew near.

26 And the watchman saw another man running and called to the gatekeeper, Behold, another man running alone. The king said, He also brings news.

27 And the watchman said, I think the running of the first is like the running of Akhima'atz the son of Tzadok. And the king said, He is a good man and comes with good news.

28 And Akhima'atz called out to the king, All is well. And he bowed before the king with his face to the earth and said, Blessed be YHWH your Creator, who has delivered up the men who raised their hand against my lord the king.

29 And the king said, Is it well with the young man Avshalom? Akhima'atz answered, When Yoav sent the king's servant, your servant, I saw a great commotion, but I do not know what it was.

30 And the king said, Turn aside and stand here. So he turned aside and stood still.

31 And behold, the Kushite came, and the Kushite said, Good news for my lord the king. For YHWH has delivered you this day from the hand of all who rose up against you.

32 The king said to the Kushite, Is it well with the young man Avshalom? And the Kushite answered, May the enemies of my lord the king and all who rise up against you for evil be like that young man.

33 And the king was deeply moved and went up to the chamber over the gate and wept. And as he went, he said, O my son Avshalom, my son, my son Avshalom. Would I had died instead of you, O Avshalom, my son, my son.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QSama preserves portions of Chapter 18. The death of Avshalom — suspended between heaven and earth in the oak, killed by Yoav against Dawid's explicit command — is the covenant tragedy at the center of Shemu'el Bet. The forest devoured more men than the sword — verse 8 — the created order fulfills the judgment that the covenant king's mercy would not.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 18 in full. Verse 33 — O my son Avshalom, my son, my son Avshalom. Would I had died instead of you — is read as the most devastating expression of parental grief in the Nevi'im. The king who could not exercise covenant justice for Avshalom's sin now mourns with an intensity that exceeds the grief for any other person in his life.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] 4QSama preserves Chapter 18 largely consistent with the Masoretic. Avshalom's monument — verse 18 — he built it because he had no son to keep his name in remembrance. The narrative noted in Chapter 14:27 that he had three sons. The contradiction suggests the sons died young. The man of magnificent appearance who stole the hearts of Yisra'EL dies tangled in an oak with no heir.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — deal gently for my sake with the young man Avshalom. The command is explicit, personal, and heard by all the troops. Yoav kills Avshalom anyway. Verse 14 — I will not waste time like this with you. Yoav's pragmatic logic overrides the king's covenant parental love. Yoav is right about the political necessity and wrong about the covenant authority. Verse 33 — the king's grief is disproportionate to the political victory because Dawid's love for Avshalom exceeded his love for the kingdom. The covenant father weeps for the covenant son he never truly reconciled with. The incomplete reconciliation of Chapter 14 produced the rebellion of Chapter 15 and the grief of Chapter 18.

END OF PART THREE — CHAPTERS 13 THROUGH 18
